findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC (or extend AC4) to verify `criterion_deltas_summary.json` is committed and conforms to the same 10-key per-criterion schema (e.g., a `jq -se` check analogous to AC4's criterion_deltas check).
evidence: Scope: '...criterion_deltas.jsonl, criterion_deltas_summary.json, fp_control.json'
Def 6: 'A rollup criterion_deltas_summary.json aggregates the same fields per criterion'
AC4 jq and pytest commands reference only criterion_deltas.jsonl — no check on the summary file
findings.finding: The plan describes `criterion_deltas_summary.json` as a committed deliverable (Scope lists it; def 6 says 'A rollup criterion_deltas_summary.json aggregates the same fields per criterion') but no acceptance criterion verifies its existence, schema, or content. AC4 only checks `criterion_deltas.jsonl`.
findings.location: Scope section (`criterion_deltas_summary.json` listing) and def 6 ('A rollup...'); AC4 (only covers criterion_deltas.jsonl)
scenarios: An implementer produces criterion_deltas.jsonl correctly but never writes the summary rollup; all ACs pass and the gap is undetected.
findings.suggested_fix: Extend AC4 with: `jq -se 'length>0 and all(.[]; has("criterion") and has("baseline_surfaced_count") and has("candidate_surfaced_count") and has("net_blocking_delta"))' docs/experiments/plan-review-gate/runs/e3/<label>/criterion_deltas_summary.json`

findings.finding: `criterion_deltas_summary.json` is a named deliverable in the Scope section ('A rollup `criterion_deltas_summary.json` aggregates the same fields per criterion') but no acceptance criterion verifies its existence, schema, or correctness. Every other output file in Scope has a covering AC; this one does not.
findings.location: Scope section ('criterion_deltas_summary.json') vs. Acceptance Criteria (AC3 covers only criterion_deltas.jsonl)
findings.suggested_fix: Append to AC3: `jq -se 'length>0 and all(.[]; has("criterion") and has("baseline_surfaced_count") and has("candidate_surfaced_count") and has("net_surfaced_delta") and has("net_blocking_delta"))' docs/experiments/plan-review-gate/runs/e3/<label>/criterion_deltas_summary.json`

findings.finding: `criterion_deltas_summary.json` is a named deliverable (described in def 6 as 'A rollup criterion_deltas_summary.json aggregates the same fields per criterion' and listed in the Scope file inventory under `<candidate>/`) but no AC verifies its existence, schema, or content.
findings.location: What § def 6 ('criterion_deltas_summary.json'); Scope file inventory; Acceptance Criteria (AC3 covers only `criterion_deltas.jsonl` and the golden test)
findings.suggested_fix: Extend AC3 with a jq one-liner: `jq -e 'has("criterion") and has("net_surfaced_delta") and has("net_blocking_delta")' docs/experiments/plan-review-gate/runs/e3/<label>/criterion_deltas_summary.json` (schema check) and a pytest golden that asserts the rollup aggregates correctly from the per-ticket rows.

findings.finding: The plan describes `criterion_deltas_summary.json` as a distinct committed artifact in both Scope ('per-candidate <candidate>/{…criterion_deltas_summary.json}') and def 6 ('A rollup criterion_deltas_summary.json aggregates the same fields per criterion'), but no Acceptance Criterion verifies this file's existence, schema, or correctness — it is an uncovered deliverable.
findings.location: Scope section (criterion_deltas_summary.json) and def 6; all 7 ACs
findings.suggested_fix: Extend AC3 to add: `jq -e 'has("criterion") and has("baseline_surfaced_count") and has("candidate_surfaced_count") and has("net_blocking_delta")' docs/experiments/plan-review-gate/runs/e3/<label>/criterion_deltas_summary.json` and add a `pytest tests/unit/test_e3_metrics.py -k summary -q` golden test covering the rollup aggregation.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Reconcile the R1 blocking fail condition: either update the parent epic's gate term to explicitly authorise `max(3, null_floor_pass_to_block)` (and re-review the parent), or constrain the leaf's `r1_pass` formula to the parent's absolute bar of 3, treating the null-floor comparison as a reported diagnostic rather than a gate override.
evidence: Parent epic: 'fail condition: >3 of 30 (10%) gain a new *blocking* finding' (SOTA §5/R1, reproduced in the epic description)
Leaf def 7: 'R1 FAILs on blocking only if `new_blocking_median > max(3, null_floor_pass_to_block)`' — introduces a dynamic floor that can exceed 3
AC6 jq: '(.r1_pass == ((.new_blocking_median <= (if 3 > .null_floor_pass_to_block then 3 else .null_floor_pass_to_block end)) and ...)' — asserts the max formula, not the parent's absolute bar of 3
G7 conflict rule: on any conflict the parent's plan is authoritative; the leaf has changed a parent AC/gate term without updating the parent first
findings.finding: The leaf redefines R1's blocking fail condition more permissively than the parent specifies. The parent epic states R1 FAILs if '>3/30 (10%) gain a new blocking finding' — an absolute threshold. The leaf replaces this with 'new_blocking_median > max(3, null_floor_pass_to_block)', so if the null floor exceeds 3, the candidate can produce 4, 5, or more tickets with new blocking findings and still pass R1. The parent's gate term is an unconditional ceiling of 3; the leaf's `max(3, null_floor)` formula overrides that ceiling upward whenever nondeterminism is high, which changes the R1 gate outcome definition in a direction the parent does not authorise.
findings.location: What § def 7 — 'R1 decision (`r1_pass`)'; AC6 jq assertion; parent epic AC3 / SOTA §5 R1 falsifiable experiment
scenarios: High nondeterminism run: null_floor=5, new_blocking_median=4 → leaf says r1_pass=True (4 ≤ max(3,5)=5); parent's absolute bar says FAIL (4 > 3). R1 ships despite violating the parent's stated gate.
Auditor or downstream R-child reads the parent epic's R1 gate terms and finds the actual E3 result was computed against a different, more permissive formula — the evidence chain is broken.
findings.suggested_fix: Move `null_floor_pass_to_block` out of the pass/fail decision and into the reported diagnostics only (it still appears in `fp_control.json` for transparency). Keep the gate formula as `r1_pass = (new_blocking_median <= 3) AND (new_advisory_median_delta <= 1)`, matching the parent's stated threshold exactly. If the null-floor argument for a dynamic threshold is compelling, update the parent epic's AC3 gate terms first, which will trigger a parent re-review, and only then re-review this leaf.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Extend AC8's grep commands to also verify that `runs/README.md` contains the over-sampling/validity-guard documentation and the R1 verdict (e.g., add `grep -q 'usable_count' ... && grep -q 'r1_pass' ...`).
evidence: Scope README bullet: 'the over-sampling drop rule + usable_count >= 30 validity guard, INDETERMINATE exclusion, the two-tier / nondeterminism / cost story, and the R1 verdict'
AC8 greps: only 'recall', 'material_fingerprint', 'cleanly-closed', 'all_review_results' — the four listed scope items above are absent from the grep set
findings.finding: AC8's grep commands verify only 4 of the 8+ README topics the Scope explicitly requires (recall, material_fingerprint, cleanly-closed, all_review_results), leaving the over-sampling ticket-drop rule, `usable_count >= 30` validity guard, R1 verdict, and two-level-median nondeterminism story unverified by any criterion.
findings.location: Scope section (README bullet: '...the over-sampling drop rule + usable_count >= 30 validity guard... the R1 verdict'); AC8 grep commands
scenarios: The README omits the validity guard rationale and R1 verdict section; all ACs pass; downstream consumers of the experiment report miss the gate decision.
findings.suggested_fix: Append to AC8: `&& grep -q 'usable_count' docs/experiments/plan-review-gate/runs/README.md && grep -q 'r1_pass' docs/experiments/plan-review-gate/runs/README.md && grep -q 'two-level' docs/experiments/plan-review-gate/runs/README.md`

findings.finding: AC7's README proving command spot-checks only four keywords (`recall`, `material_fingerprint`, `cleanly-closed`, `all_review_results`) but the Scope section requires the README to cover six distinct topics: baseline source + chosen round, cleanly-closed filter, recall-strip + fresh-both-gates design, material-fingerprint drift control, two-tier LLM-nondeterminism/cost story, and the R1 verdict. Two of the six required topics ('two-tier cost story' and 'R1 verdict') have no grep anchor in the proving command, so AC7 can be satisfied by a README that omits them.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC7 (README documentation)
findings.suggested_fix: Append to the AC7 proving command: `&& grep -q 'two-tier' docs/experiments/plan-review-gate/runs/README.md && grep -q 'r1_pass' docs/experiments/plan-review-gate/runs/README.md`

evidence: description is 27776 chars (> 24000)
findings.finding: Oversize signals suggest this unit may be too large for one session.
findings.suggested_fix: Split into smaller child tickets, each a coherent single outcome.

evidence: 2 criterion line(s) bundle multiple deliverables with 'and' (split so each is independently verifiable).
vague/subjective terms present: clean
findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a proving command in AC1 (or a new AC) that verifies every `ticket_id` in `fp_control_pool.jsonl` also appears in `sample.jsonl`, e.g. `python -c "import json; s={json.loads(l)['ticket_id'] for l in open('runs/e3/sample.jsonl')}; p=[json.loads(l)['ticket_id'] for l in open('runs/e3/fp_control_pool.jsonl')]; assert all(t in s for t in p), 'pool not a subset of sample'; print('SUBSET-OK')"`
evidence: "FP-control pool = deterministic `random.Random(1729)` draw of 50 from the `baseline_verdict == PASS` members of the 200-sample (a genuine subset)"
AC1 checks only `wc -l ... -eq 50` (cardinality) and the cleanly-closed filter, not the subset relationship.
No other AC contains a `jq` or Python check of the form 'every pool ticket_id appears in sample.jsonl'.
findings.finding: No acceptance criterion verifies that `fp_control_pool.jsonl` is a genuine subset of `sample.jsonl` — a property the plan asserts explicitly ('a genuine subset') — leaving the key structural invariant between the two tiers unverified by any proving command.
findings.location: What / Stratified sampling frame; AC1 proving command
scenarios: A bug in `replay_candidate.py sample` draws the FP-control pool independently rather than from the 200-sample, silently violating the two-tier design's 'genuine subset' invariant. The current ACs would not catch this.
The coarse tier's flip-rate denominator and the FP-control tier's scored set quietly diverge, invalidating the 'corroborating aggregate' relationship between the two metrics.
findings.suggested_fix: Append to AC1's proving block: `python -c "import json; s={json.loads(l)['ticket_id'] for l in open('docs/experiments/plan-review-gate/runs/e3/sample.jsonl')}; bad=[json.loads(l)['ticket_id'] for l in open('docs/experiments/plan-review-gate/runs/e3/fp_control_pool.jsonl') if json.loads(l)['ticket_id'] not in s]; assert not bad, f'pool tickets not in sample: {bad}'; print('POOL-SUBSET-OK')"`.

findings.finding: The plan specifies 'atomic append per row (checkpoint/resume)' for raw_verdicts.jsonl but does not specify the atomicity mechanism (O_APPEND, file lock, etc.), nor does it specify how the driver avoids duplicate (ticket_id, replay_index) rows when a run is resumed after interruption. Without an explicit deduplication guard or idempotency check, a resumed run will append already-completed rows a second time, silently corrupting the JSONL input to the analyze step.
findings.location: What section — 'The replay (candidate side)' paragraph; Scope — harnesses/replay_candidate.py
findings.suggested_fix: Use Python's O_APPEND open mode for line atomicity on POSIX. Before appending each row, check whether a row with the same (ticket_id, replay_index) already exists in the file (load existing keys at driver startup into a set); skip if already present. Document this in the driver spec.

findings.finding: The `replay_candidate.py analyze --candidate <label> --verify` sub-command's exit-code / failure behavior on assertion failure is never specified. The `sample --verify` command is explicitly stated to 'exit non-zero unless byte-identical to committed', but `analyze --verify` only says it 'recomputes `transition_matrix.json` from committed `raw_verdicts.jsonl` + `baseline.jsonl`, asserts equality' — 'asserts equality' is ambiguous about the output contract (exit code, error message, diff output).
findings.location: AC2 proving command description
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the AC2 description: '(offline; recomputes `transition_matrix.json` from committed `raw_verdicts.jsonl` + `baseline.jsonl`, exits non-zero and prints a diff unless byte-identical to committed)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State a concrete enlargement rule for the FP-control pool re-draw (e.g., 'add `ceil(deficit / 0.61)` tickets to the pool, capped at the full PASS-baseline corpus, then re-freeze `fp_control_pool.jsonl`') so the recovery path is deterministic.
evidence: "a run is INVALID only if even the 50-pool cannot yield 30 fully-usable tickets (`usable_count < 30`), in which case it is re-run after enlarging/re-freezing the pool and yields no R1 verdict"
No formula or increment for the enlarged pool size is given.
findings.finding: The recovery path when `fp_control_valid` is False (i.e., `usable_count < 30`) says "re-run after enlarging/re-freezing the pool" but gives no concrete rule for HOW MUCH to enlarge the pool — the enlargement quantum is fully deferred to the executor.
findings.location: What / def 8 (Over-sampling ticket-drop rule + FP-control validity guard)
scenarios: The executor sees usable_count=28 and must decide: add 5 tickets? 20? Redraw entirely? Without a stated rule, two executors make different choices, making the experiment non-reproducible on retry.
If the INDETERMINATE rate is higher than expected (e.g. 15%), successive re-runs with ad-hoc enlargements may never converge.
findings.suggested_fix: In def 8, append: "If `fp_control_valid` is False after a run, increase the pool to `ceil(30 / P_usable)` where `P_usable` is the empirically observed `usable_count / pool_size` from that run (minimum pool size 50, maximum the full PASS-baseline corpus), re-freeze `fp_control_pool.jsonl`, and re-run. If the full corpus is exhausted, the experiment is inconclusive and must be escalated."

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Acknowledge `sidecar.fix_unit_key()` in def 6/def 7 and explicitly justify why `tkey` is defined separately rather than reusing it — specifically document that `location` is intentionally excluded from `tkey` (and why: cross-run location-string instability) so the executor can verify the design choice rather than guess.
evidence: Plan def 6: `tkey(f) = "t" + sha256(" ".join(sorted({tok for tok in re.findall(r"[a-z0-9]+", f["finding"].lower()) if len(tok) > 3})))[:16]` — hashes only `finding` field
sidecar.py:279-290: `fix_unit_key` = criteria-free hash of `_norm_tokens(location) + "|" + _norm_tokens(finding)` — identical token recipe, also criteria-free, but includes `location`
sidecar.py:280-283 docstring: 'CRITERIA-FREE fix-unit fingerprint: same-location + same-claim findings share it even when different criteria cite them' — same motivating rationale as tkey
No mention of `fix_unit_key` anywhere in the plan text — absence confirmed by search
findings.finding: The plan defines a new `tkey` function to be implemented in `e3_metrics.py` as a criteria-free significant-token hash of finding text. However, `sidecar.fix_unit_key(finding)` (sidecar.py:279-290) already exists in the production codebase and serves a nearly identical purpose — it is explicitly documented as 'A CRITERIA-FREE fix-unit fingerprint: same-location + same-claim findings share it even when different criteria cite them' using the same token-normalization recipe. The only structural difference is that `fix_unit_key` includes the `location` field in its hash while `tkey` hashes only `finding` text. The plan never acknowledges `fix_unit_key`'s existence, never explains why a new function is preferred over the existing one, and never justifies the location-exclusion design choice. An executor implementing `e3_metrics.py` cannot determine from the plan text whether to reuse `fix_unit_key` or define `tkey` fresh — and if `fix_unit_key` is in fact correct for this use case, defining `tkey` as a divergent rebuild creates a permanent mismatch between E3's matching semantics and any future code that joins on `fix_unit_key`.
findings.location: def 6 / def 7 (`tkey` definition); Scope (`e3_metrics.py`); sidecar.py:279-290
scenarios: Executor implements tkey as specified (text-only), unaware fix_unit_key exists; two location-varying runs of the same finding produce the same tkey correctly (location omission may be intentional for cross-run stability) — but the rationale is absent, so the executor may wrongly defer to fix_unit_key and include location, inflating new-finding counts.
A future maintainer extending e3_metrics.py finds tkey and fix_unit_key with identical recipes but different scopes and no explanation; silent divergence grows.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to def 6: 'Note: the codebase already has `sidecar.fix_unit_key(f)`, a criteria-free hash of `(location, finding text)`. `tkey` intentionally excludes `location` because the same gate run can format a finding's location differently across replays (path:line vs prose name), which would spuriously inflate new-finding counts. `e3_metrics.tkey` is therefore a standalone function; do not replace it with `fix_unit_key`.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Move the zero-denominator guard from AC5 jq into def 5's normative formula — explicitly state `flip_rate = cells.pass_to_block / max(1, cells.pass_to_pass + cells.pass_to_block)` (or equivalent) so `e3_metrics.pass_to_block_flip_rate` is fully specified without reading the AC.
evidence: Plan def 5: 'flip_rate = cells.pass_to_block / (cells.pass_to_pass + cells.pass_to_block)' — no zero-denominator handling mentioned
Plan AC5 jq: '(.cells.pass_to_pass + .cells.pass_to_block) | if .==0 then 1 else . end' — zero-denominator guard present in the AC only
Scope: `e3_metrics.pass_to_block_flip_rate` is a pure function to be implemented from the plan spec — its author reads def 5, not AC5
findings.finding: The flip-rate zero-denominator guard (`if .==0 then 1 else . end`) is specified only inside the AC5 jq proving command, not in the normative def 5 text where the flip_rate formula lives. Def 5 states `flip_rate = cells.pass_to_block / (cells.pass_to_pass + cells.pass_to_block)` with no mention of the zero-denominator case. The processing rule (substitute 1 for a zero denominator) is thus co-located only with the AC assertion, not with the schema definition where the `e3_metrics.pass_to_block_flip_rate` function would be implemented. An executor writing `pass_to_block_flip_rate` from def 5 alone would produce a ZeroDivisionError or NaN when the PASS-baseline count is zero.
findings.location: def 5 (`flip_rate` formula); AC5 (jq proving command)
scenarios: A corpus segment where all cleanly-closed sampled tickets historically BLOCKed (unlikely but not impossible for a small ticket_type cell) yields denominator=0; the pure function raises ZeroDivisionError rather than returning 0.0 or a sentinel.

The AC5 jq substitutes 1 for a zero denominator (making flip_rate=0), but the golden test in `test_e3_metrics.py` is written from def 5 and omits the zero-denominator case — AC proves jq passes but doesn't prove the Python function handles it.
findings.suggested_fix: In def 5, replace the formula with: `flip_rate = cells.pass_to_block / max(1, cells.pass_to_pass + cells.pass_to_block)` and add: '(If the PASS-baseline count is zero — no PASS-baseline tickets in this segment — flip_rate is defined as 0.0 and the segment is noted as degenerate.)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define a RETIRE/cleanup path for the recall-stripped tmp clone in replay_candidate.py: specify when the clone is deleted (e.g., on clean exit, via `--cleanup` flag, or via a registered atexit handler) and document it in the Scope section.
evidence: 'copy REAL_REPO/.tickets-tracker → a tmp store, then delete every *-REVIEW_RESULT.json file under each sampled ticket's dir in the clone'
No mention of cleanup, teardown, or tmp-dir management anywhere in What, Scope, or AC sections
The driver is described as supporting checkpoint/resume (raw_verdicts.jsonl 'atomic append per row (checkpoint/resume)'), implying multiple partial runs — each of which creates a new clone
findings.finding: The recall-stripped tmp clone created in def 3 has no defined RETIRE/cleanup path. The plan says 'copy REAL_REPO/.tickets-tracker → a tmp store' and sets REBAR_TRACKER_DIR to the clone, but never specifies when or how the tmp directory is removed. On repeated invocations (retry after LLM outage, re-running for a second candidate, or the driver crashing mid-run), each call creates a new clone that is never cleaned up, causing unbounded disk accumulation in the tmp filesystem.
findings.location: def 3 (Recall-stripped clone) and Scope → replay_candidate.py driver description
scenarios: An operator re-runs replay_candidate.py run after an LLM outage mid-way through 200 tickets; each invocation creates a fresh clone but the prior clone is never deleted, leaving multiple multi-GB copies of .tickets-tracker on the executor's disk.
findings.suggested_fix: In replay_candidate.py, wrap the run subcommand in a try/finally that calls shutil.rmtree on the tmp clone dir on clean exit; for checkpoint/resume, canonicalize the tmp path as `<runs/e3>/<label>/clone/` so reruns reuse the same dir (and re-strip on each run) rather than creating a new one each time.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add unit/integration tests (with a mock `review_plan`) for `replay_candidate.py`'s driver failure paths: partial-batch resume (some rows committed, crash mid-run), clone-strip verification (assert REVIEW_RESULT files are absent post-strip), `impact_model_version` mismatch assertion, and the `--verify` re-derivation mode on a synthetic corpus fixture.
evidence: Plan names `replay_candidate.py` as covering `sample / run --candidate <label> / run --main / analyze --candidate <label> / --verify, incl. clone + REVIEW_RESULT strip + manifest write`
Tests section (`tests/unit/test_e3_metrics.py`) covers only pure `e3_metrics` functions; no driver tests are mentioned anywhere in the plan
Plan mentions `atomic append per row (checkpoint/resume)` as a driver behavior with no corresponding test
Plan mentions driver asserts `sidecar.IMPACT_MODEL_VERSION` equals cohort version and `fails loud otherwise` — no test for this failure branch
findings.finding: The driver `replay_candidate.py` — a new user-facing CLI with `sample`, `run --candidate`, `run --main`, `analyze`, and `--verify` subcommands — has no specified failure-path or error-path tests. Its non-trivial stateful behaviors (atomic-append checkpoint/resume on crash mid-batch, clone creation and REVIEW_RESULT strip, `source='local'` requirement for `review_plan`, manifest write, and the `--verify` re-derivation mode) are exercised only via the AC proving commands on committed artifacts, which are happy-path-only. Failure modes — corpus file missing/malformed, incomplete replay (some rows written, crash before completion), clone-strip failure, `review_plan` raising an exception vs. returning INDETERMINATE, mismatched `impact_model_version` assert-loud path — have no test coverage specified.
findings.location: Scope section (`harnesses/replay_candidate.py`) and AC3 (replay coverage assertion)
scenarios: Driver crashes mid-batch after writing 150/200 rows; resume behavior is untested — could double-count or skip rows
Clone strip silently fails (permissions); driver proceeds with recall contamination, silently producing invalid results
`review_plan` raises an exception (not INDETERMINATE verdict) mid-batch; driver's exception handling is unspecified and untested
The `--verify` subcommand re-derives byte-identically only when the corpus hasn't changed; a malformed corpus row is silently skipped or raises uncaught
findings.suggested_fix: Create `tests/unit/test_replay_driver.py` that patches `review_plan` with a controllable fake (raises, returns INDETERMINATE, returns PASS/BLOCK) and exercises: (1) atomic-append idempotency on resume after partial write, (2) clone-strip leaving zero `*-REVIEW_RESULT.json` files, (3) `impact_model_version` mismatch causing a `SystemExit`/raised exception, (4) `--verify` passing on a known-good synthetic sample and failing on a mutated one.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State explicitly that `outcome_corpus.jsonl` is committed to the repo and give its path relative to the repo root (e.g. `docs/experiments/plan-review-gate/runs/outcome_corpus.jsonl`) so the `sample --verify` command is provably offline-runnable.
evidence: "an explicit filter over `outcome_corpus.jsonl` (628 rows; schema in `runs/README.md:53-88`)"
AC1: `python ... replay_candidate.py sample --verify` described as '(offline; re-derives sample + baseline, exits non-zero unless byte-identical to committed)'
The plan never states 'outcome_corpus.jsonl is committed at <path>' or 'generated by <prior step>'
findings.finding: `outcome_corpus.jsonl` (628 rows) is referenced as the source for deterministic sampling and the offline `sample --verify` command in AC1, but the plan never states that this file is committed to the repository or specifies its path relative to the repo root. If it is an external or generated artifact, the AC1 `--verify` proving command — described as 'offline' — has a missing prerequisite that would silently prevent in-session verification.
findings.location: What / Definitions / cleanly-closed ticket (def 1); AC1 proving command
scenarios: Executor attempts to run AC1 `--verify` offline but `outcome_corpus.jsonl` is absent from the clone, causing an unresolvable file-not-found error with no fallback stated in the plan.
A reviewer cannot confirm AC1 is genuinely offline-reproducible without knowing the file's committed location.
findings.suggested_fix: Add one sentence in def 1 or the Scope section: "`outcome_corpus.jsonl` is a committed repo artifact at `docs/experiments/plan-review-gate/runs/outcome_corpus.jsonl` (628 rows; do not regenerate — treat as read-only ground truth for all sampling steps)."

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify retry/backoff policy (e.g., exponential backoff with N retries on transient HTTP errors / 429s) for each LLM call in the batch driver, and clarify whether a retried-then-succeeded call is recorded as PASS/BLOCK (not INDETERMINATE) — so the ~8% INDETERMINATE rate assumption is grounded in post-retry behavior.
evidence: 'INDETERMINATE (LLM outage / mid-run degradation — §5.1: ~8% of reviews)' — cited as known figure but no retry policy stated
'A ticket is UNUSABLE if ANY of its 3 candidate OR 3 current replays returns an INDETERMINATE verdict (any reason)' — aggregates across all causes without distinguishing transient vs permanent
'run once per candidate/main by the executor' — no retry/backoff/circuit-breaker mentioned for the batch driver
ABSENCE: no mention of retry count, backoff policy, or rate-limit handling in replay_candidate.py driver spec
findings.finding: The plan specifies that transient LLM failures during the batch run are handled at the analysis level (INDETERMINATE verdict → excluded/dropped) but never specifies whether the driver retries transient errors (network blips, rate-limit 429s, provider timeouts) before recording INDETERMINATE. This matters because the ~8% INDETERMINATE rate is load-bearing: it drives the pool-size calculation (50 over-sample to yield ≥30 usable), and if real transient-retry behavior differs from the assumed ~8%, the validity guard may trigger more often than expected, requiring re-runs.
findings.location: ## What — 'The replay (candidate side)' paragraph; def 8 'Over-sampling ticket-drop rule'; §5.1 ~8% INDETERMINATE citation
scenarios: Provider returns 429 rate-limit mid-batch; driver records INDETERMINATE for those tickets; unusable count spikes above the 39% estimate; `fp_control_valid` fails and the whole run must be re-executed rather than resumed from the point of failure
Transient network blip during the main gate's 3 replays causes a pool ticket to be dropped; scored_count falls below 30 unexpectedly
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the driver spec: 'Each `review_plan` call is wrapped with up to 3 exponential-backoff retries on transient errors (429, 5xx, network timeout ≤ 30 s); only a call that exhausts retries is recorded as INDETERMINATE. The ~8% INDETERMINATE estimate is post-retry.' This grounds the pool-size calculation and makes the retry contract explicit.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Collect all R1 gate threshold literals (`BLOCKING_BAR=3`, `ADVISORY_BAR=1`, `SCORED_NEED=30`, `POOL_SIZE=50`, `FLIP_RATE_BAR=0.02`) into a single named-constants section of `e3_metrics.py` (or a small `e3_constants.py`), and reference those names from the driver, fp_control_eval, and the AC jq/Python proof commands — so a SOTA spec update requires one change, not a scatter update.
evidence: 'R1 FAILs if ... >3/30 (10%) gain a new blocking finding' — literal 3 in fp_control_eval and AC jq
'fp_control_valid(usable_count, need=30) -> bool' — need=30 is a parameter here but 30 also appears as literal in AC jq `(.scored_count==30)`
'pool_size (int, 50)' appears in fp_control.json schema and implicitly in the pool-draw logic
AC jq: `(.scored_count==30)` and `if 3 > .null_floor_pass_to_block then 3` — thresholds duplicated in proof commands
ABSENCE: no mention of a constants module or config file collecting these gate thresholds
findings.finding: The R1 gate thresholds (`3` blocking bar, `1` advisory delta bar, `30` scored count, `50` pool size, `0.02` flip-rate bar) appear as literals scattered across `fp_control_eval`, the batch driver, and the AC jq assertions. When the SOTA spec evolves (e.g., the blocking bar rises from 3 to 4), all sites must be updated in sync; the plan provides no named-constant or config-file mechanism to keep them consistent.
findings.location: ## What — def 7 ('r1_pass' formula), def 8 ('pool of 50', 'need=30'), AC6 jq assertion; Scope — e3_metrics.py
scenarios: SOTA spec raises the blocking bar from 3 to 4; developer updates fp_control_eval but misses the jq AC or the driver's pool-size formula; tests pass but the gate silently applies the old bar
The scored-count requirement changes; the jq AC and fp_control_valid's need= parameter diverge
findings.suggested_fix: In `e3_metrics.py` add a top-level block: `BLOCKING_BAR = 3; ADVISORY_BAR = 1; SCORED_NEED = 30; POOL_SIZE = 50; FLIP_RATE_BAR = 0.02` and reference these in `fp_control_eval` and `fp_control_valid`. Update AC jq proofs to use `env` vars or note which constant they encode, so reviewers can audit consistency mechanically.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the atomicity mechanism for raw_verdicts.jsonl appends (e.g., single-writer process, per-row temp-file rename, or advisory file lock) and state whether the driver uses sequential or parallel execution of gate calls.
evidence: 'capturing each returned verdict dict … to runs/e3/<candidate>/raw_verdicts.jsonl, each row keyed by (ticket_id, replay_index), atomic append per row (checkpoint/resume)'
The plan names harnesses/run_review_*.py as the 'batch-driver precedent' but does not describe its concurrency model or assert it is serial
~200 tickets × 3 FP-pool replays = up to 650 gate calls; running these serially would take hours, making parallelism a near-certain implementation choice
Python's file.write in append mode is not atomic across processes without OS-level advisory locking
findings.finding: The plan claims 'atomic append per row' for raw_verdicts.jsonl but specifies no concurrency-safety mechanism. For ~200 tickets, parallel execution of review_plan calls is the natural and cost-efficient implementation, yet without a named locking or serialization strategy (file lock, temp-file rename, single-writer queue), concurrent appends from multiple worker processes would silently interleave or corrupt JSONL rows, and the checkpoint/resume guarantee would be violated.
findings.location: 'The replay (candidate side)' section: 'atomic append per row (checkpoint/resume)' and Scope → replay_candidate.py driver
scenarios: A driver implementation that uses a ThreadPoolExecutor or multiprocessing pool (the natural choice for throughput) has two workers simultaneously appending to raw_verdicts.jsonl; without a file lock, the two JSON objects can be interleaved, producing unparseable rows that silently fail the completion assertion in AC3.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace POSIX-only shell AC proving commands (head, wc -l, test with $(...), grep -q chains) with cross-platform equivalents — either inline Python one-liners (as AC3 already does) or pytest-based checks — so ACs can be proved on Windows without a POSIX compatibility layer.
evidence: AC1: 'test $(wc -l < docs/experiments/plan-review-gate/runs/e3/fp_control_pool.jsonl) -eq 50'
AC1: "jq -e 'select(.post_claim_edit_class!="none" or .reopen_count!=0 or .force_close!=false)' … | head -c1 | ( ! grep -q . )"
AC7: 'grep -q "recall" docs/experiments/plan-review-gate/runs/README.md && grep -q "material_fingerprint" … && grep -q "cleanly-closed" … && grep -q "all_review_results" …'
Supported platform matrix includes 'Windows' under 'Platform and venue: macOS, Windows, Linux, BSD, CI, servers, developer workstations'
head, wc, and the POSIX subshell negation pattern ( ! … ) are unavailable in Windows cmd.exe and produce errors in native PowerShell without Git Bash or WSL
findings.finding: Every AC proving command that uses POSIX shell utilities (head -c1, wc -l, test with $(...) substitution, grep -q with negation subshell) cannot be executed on Windows without a POSIX compatibility layer. Windows is an explicitly supported platform in the rebar support matrix, and a developer or CI runner on Windows would be unable to prove any of the shell-based ACs.
findings.location: AC1 proving command: 'test $(wc -l < .../fp_control_pool.jsonl) -eq 50' and 'jq … | head -c1 | ( ! grep -q . )'; AC7: 'grep -q "recall" … && grep -q …'
scenarios: A developer on a Windows workstation running the AC1 proof command in cmd.exe or PowerShell receives ''wc' is not recognized as an internal or external command' and cannot verify the 50-row FP-control pool invariant, making the AC unprovable on a supported platform.

A Windows CI runner (e.g., GitHub Actions windows-latest) executing the AC7 grep chain fails with a non-zero exit code because grep is not in PATH, causing the CI job to report AC7 as failed even when the README content is correct.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'test $(wc -l < fp_control_pool.jsonl) -eq 50' with 'python -c "assert sum(1 for _ in open(\"…/fp_control_pool.jsonl\")) == 50"'; replace the head/grep negation idiom with 'python -c "assert not any(…)"'; replace grep -q chains with 'python -c "text=open(…).read(); assert all(k in text for k in [\"recall\",…])"'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] In def 6/def 7, explicitly compare `tkey` to the existing `sidecar.fix_unit_key` and document why `tkey` excludes `location` from the hash basis (intentional design choice for cross-run stability vs accidental omission).
evidence: Plan def 6: `tkey(f) = "t" + sha256(" ".join(sorted({tok for tok in re.findall(r"[a-z0-9]+", f["finding"].lower()) if len(tok) > 3})))[:16]` — finding text only
sidecar.py:279-290: `fix_unit_key` = criteria-free hash of `_norm_tokens(location) + "|" + _norm_tokens(finding)` — location included

sidecar.py:280-283: docstring explicitly states 'CRITERIA-FREE fix-unit fingerprint: same-location + same-claim findings share it even when different criteria cite them' — same motivating rationale as tkey
No acknowledgment of fix_unit_key anywhere in the plan text
findings.finding: The plan defines a new `tkey` function in `e3_metrics.py` that computes a criteria-free significant-token hash of finding text — but the codebase already contains `sidecar.fix_unit_key(finding)` (src/rebar/llm/plan_review/sidecar.py:279-290), which is also a criteria-free, token-normalized hash serving the same 'same defect, different criterion attribution' use-case. The plan's `tkey` differs from `fix_unit_key` in one undiscussed dimension: `fix_unit_key` hashes `(location + finding text)` while `tkey` hashes only `finding text`. That design choice may be intentional (location strings differ more across runs than text) but the plan never acknowledges `fix_unit_key`'s existence or explains the divergence — leaving the reader unable to verify whether this is an intentional scoping difference or an accidental rebuild.
findings.location: def 6 / def 7, `tkey` definition; `e3_metrics.py` scope entry
scenarios: If the location exclusion in tkey is wrong (two runs of the same gate can surface the same finding with slightly different location strings, making tkey correct to ignore location), then fix_unit_key would over-count new findings — but this reasoning is absent from the plan.
If a future maintainer extends tkey or fix_unit_key without knowing both exist, they diverge silently.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a sentence in def 6: 'Unlike `sidecar.fix_unit_key`, `tkey` omits `location` from the hash because independently-run criteria finders format the same location string differently across builds, so location-inclusion would inflate false new-finding counts. `fix_unit_key` is not used here for this reason.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the `plan-review-chunking-budget.md` reference in def 7 to point to the actual existing document (likely `docs/experiments/plan-review-model-tradeoff.md` or `docs/experiments/plan-review-criteria-storage-and-chunking.md`) that records the default-temperature observation.
evidence: Search for 'plan-review-chunking-budget.md' finds zero direct hits; only 'docs/experiments/plan-review-model-tradeoff.md' mentions it as a referenced predecessor.
Files docs/experiments/plan-review-criteria-storage-and-chunking.md and docs/experiments/plan-review-model-tradeoff.md exist but neither matches the cited name.
findings.finding: The plan cites `plan-review-chunking-budget.md` as the document that 'notes default temperature for natural repeat-variance', but no file by that exact name exists in the repository. The closest files are `docs/experiments/plan-review-criteria-storage-and-chunking.md` and `docs/experiments/plan-review-model-tradeoff.md` (which references the chunking-budget doc as a predecessor). This cited document either has a different name or is absent.
findings.location: def 7 / LLM nondeterminism control section: 'plan-review-chunking-budget.md notes default temperature for natural repeat-variance'
scenarios: A reviewer or implementer who tries to find the cited document to confirm the temperature claim will not find it, potentially leading them to question whether the nondeterminism justification is grounded.
If the document was renamed or never committed, the temperature/seed rationale for N=3 replication has no verifiable backing.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the citation with the actual file path: `docs/experiments/plan-review-model-tradeoff.md` (which discusses the model tradeoff experiment that followed the chunking-budget analysis) or whichever file contains the temperature observation.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit priority rule stating that `r1_pass` from `fp_control.json` (precise tier) is the sole decisive R1 gate criterion, and that the coarse-tier flip rate in `transition_matrix.json` is strictly supplementary/descriptive — including guidance on how to interpret a coarse-tier result that exceeds ≤2% due to the acknowledged recall-asymmetry confound.
evidence: "Epic additionally reports a corroborating aggregate: the PASS→BLOCK flip rate ≤2% on the wider cleanly-closed sample."
"The matrix is … reported with the coarse-tier caveat" — caveat acknowledged but no priority rule stated.
AC5 jq asserts flip_rate correctness in transition_matrix.json but does not state whether this is a hard gate or supplementary.
AC6 asserts r1_pass from fp_control.json (precise tier) is the decisive verdict — but this hierarchy is implicit, never declared in the plan.
findings.finding: The plan reports the coarse-tier PASS→BLOCK flip rate under the epic's ≤2% bar but simultaneously acknowledges that the coarse tier has a systematic upward bias (the persisted baseline carried recall that the fresh candidate run does not, inflating PASS→BLOCK flips even for a benign candidate). The plan does not specify the decision rule when coarse-tier and precise-tier results conflict — e.g., what the gate operator should conclude if the coarse tier shows 3% but `fp_control.json` returns `r1_pass=true`. Without this explicit priority rule, a confound-inflated coarse-tier failure could be mistakenly treated as a real gate failure, or vice versa.
findings.location: Why section ("corroborating aggregate: the PASS→BLOCK flip rate ≤2%") and Def 5 / AC5 (flip_rate in transition_matrix.json)
scenarios: A benign candidate is assessed: the coarse tier shows 3% flips (recall confound inflating the count), while the precise tier correctly shows r1_pass=true. The gate operator has no documented rule for which metric wins and may incorrectly block the candidate.

A harmful candidate is assessed: the precise tier shows r1_pass=false, but the coarse tier happens to show 1.5% (low due to sampling variance). Without explicit hierarchy, a reader might weigh the coarse-tier result as exculpatory.
findings.suggested_fix: In the 'Why' section and README E3 documentation, add a sentence such as: 'The R1 gate verdict is determined solely by `r1_pass` in `fp_control.json` (precise tier). The coarse-tier flip rate in `transition_matrix.json` is supplementary; a coarse-tier result exceeding ≤2% does not constitute a gate failure and should be interpreted in light of the recall-asymmetry caveat before concluding candidate impact.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Justify the two-level median design (N=3 replays, median-over-30) against established alternatives — at minimum cite why paired Wilcoxon, McNemar's test, or bootstrap CI were not used, and include or reference a power analysis for N=3.
evidence: 'the reported metric = the MEDIAN over the 30 tickets' — chosen with no comparison to Wilcoxon/McNemar/bootstrap
'N=3' — no power analysis cited for this replication count
No prior art referenced for the two-level median design pattern
findings.finding: The two-level median statistical design (median-of-3-paired-replays → median-over-30-tickets) is a novel approach to controlling LLM nondeterminism with no stated comparison to established alternatives — paired Wilcoxon signed-rank test, McNemar's test for binary flip counts, or bootstrap confidence intervals. With N=3 replays and a 30-ticket scored set, statistical power is very limited and the choice of statistic is load-bearing for the R1 gate decision. The plan offers no justification for the median-of-medians over these established methods.
findings.location: Def 7: 'LLM nondeterminism control — replication, pairing, and the null floor (precise tier)'
scenarios: The median-of-3 is just the middle value; with N=3 a single outlier pair controls the result — a mean or rank-based test would be more robust
A reviewer or auditor of the gate decision has no basis to trust the statistical design over a paired t-test or bootstrap CI
If the true INDETERMINATE rate is higher than 8%, N=3 may leave many tickets with only 1 or 2 usable pairs, making the median unstable

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Acknowledge and address the independence assumption in the `0.92^6` usability probability — either cite evidence that INDETERMINATE events are uncorrelated across replays of the same ticket, or widen the pool size conservatively (e.g., 70–80) to hedge against clustered outages.
evidence: 'P(ticket usable) ≈ 0.92^6 ≈ 0.61' — independence assumed, not stated or justified
'~8% of reviews' cited from §5.1 as the INDETERMINATE rate — but INDETERMINATE events during a replay session are likely correlated
're-run after enlarging/re-freezing the pool' — the only recovery path is post-hoc
findings.finding: The oversampling calculation `P(ticket usable) ≈ 0.92^6 ≈ 0.61` (requiring all 6 replays — 3 candidate + 3 current — to be non-INDETERMINATE) assumes INDETERMINATE events are statistically independent across replays of the same ticket. This assumption is unverified: LLM outages, rate limits, and infrastructure degradation are positively correlated within a short time window, meaning runs of the same ticket in the same session are NOT independent. If the true per-ticket usability probability is lower (e.g., 0.5 when outages cluster), the 50-ticket pool may not yield 30 usable tickets reliably, invalidating the run without a clear recovery path beyond 're-run after enlarging/re-freezing the pool'.
findings.location: Def 8: 'Over-sampling ticket-drop rule + FP-control validity guard (precise tier)'; also Def 7 cost discussion
scenarios: A sustained partial LLM outage during the replay session causes 40%+ of tickets to fail at least one replay — the 50-pool yields <30 usable tickets and the run must be redone
The 8% rate from §5.1 reflects historical production conditions; a fresh recall-stripped run hitting a cold LLM endpoint may have a different INDETERMINATE rate

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the checkpoint/resume protocol for `replay_index` assignment: define whether `replay_index` is a CLI parameter (e.g. `--replay 1`), a loop counter, or auto-inferred from existing rows on resume — and state explicitly how a mid-batch restart skips already-written `(ticket_id, replay_index)` pairs.
evidence: Plan: 'capturing each returned verdict dict ... to runs/e3/<candidate>/raw_verdicts.jsonl, each row keyed by (ticket_id, replay_index), atomic append per row (checkpoint/resume)'
Plan: 'Replication is TIERED ... the coarse ~200 sample runs ONCE (replay_index=1); the ~50-ticket FP-control pool additionally runs replay_index ∈ {2,3}'
Plan: 'Completion assertion before any summary: every one of the ~200 sample tickets has a replay_index=1 row' — implies the driver re-checks on resume
No specification of how replay_index is set during execution or how a partial file is interpreted on resume
findings.finding: The plan specifies `replay_index` as a per-row key in `raw_verdicts.jsonl` and describes atomic-append checkpoint/resume behavior, but never specifies how the driver assigns or increments `replay_index` during execution, nor how a resumed run determines which `replay_index` to continue from. The plan says 'atomic append per row (checkpoint/resume)' and 'rows keyed by (ticket_id, replay_index)' but is silent on whether replay_index is a CLI argument, a loop counter reset on each run, or inferred from existing rows. If the driver is re-launched after a partial crash, it needs to determine both which tickets still need `replay_index=1` rows and (for pool tickets) which of `{1,2,3}` are still missing — but the resume protocol is not defined.
findings.location: 'The replay (candidate side)' section; AC3 proving command
scenarios: Driver crashes after writing 120/200 replay_index=1 rows. On resume, driver scans existing rows to find which tickets still need replay_index=1 — but this scan logic is never specified; an executor might restart the entire batch, producing duplicate rows for the 120 already done, breaking the keyed invariant.
Pool ticket has replay_index=1 written but not {2,3}; resume policy for partial pool tickets is undefined.

findings.finding: The prose completion assertion — 'every one of the ~200 sample tickets has a replay_index=1 row, and every FP-pool ticket has rows for all of replay_index ∈ {1,2,3}' — appears only in the body text with no corresponding AC proving command. All downstream offline ACs (AC2–AC5) re-derive summaries from raw_verdicts.jsonl, so if that file is incomplete (e.g., the live run was interrupted), the analysis silently runs over partial data and the ACs still pass.
findings.location: Scope / 'The replay (candidate side)' section; Acceptance Criteria (no AC covers this)
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC2 (or as a new AC before AC2): `python docs/experiments/plan-review-gate/harnesses/replay_candidate.py run --candidate <label> --verify-complete` (or an equivalent jq/awk one-liner such as: `jq -r 'select(.replay_index==1)|.ticket_id' runs/e3/<label>/raw_verdicts.jsonl | sort > /tmp/cand_ids.txt && jq -r '.ticket_id' runs/e3/sample.jsonl | sort > /tmp/sample_ids.txt && diff /tmp/sample_ids.txt /tmp/cand_ids.txt` plus an analogous check for the FP-pool replay_index ∈ {1,2,3}).

findings.finding: The `transition_matrix.json` schema used in AC2's jq proving command references a field named `.observed_total`, but this field is never defined by name in the plan's 'Verdict → matrix mapping' section (def 2 / the matrix schema). The plan describes the consistency invariant as `cells.pass_to_pass + cells.pass_to_block + cells.block_to_pass + cells.block_to_block + excluded.total` equalling the row total, but the field name an executor must emit to satisfy the AC2 jq (`(.cells.pass_to_pass + .cells.pass_to_block + .cells.block_to_pass + .cells.block_to_block + .excluded.total) == .observed_total`) is never declared in the schema definition.
findings.location: Def 2 / 'Verdict → matrix mapping' section and AC2 jq proving command
findings.suggested_fix: In the 'Verdict → matrix mapping' definition, add: `observed_total` (int) = the count of tickets processed for this segment (= `cells.pass_to_pass + cells.pass_to_block + cells.block_to_pass + cells.block_to_block + excluded.total`). This makes the AC2 jq provably executable.

findings.finding: The plan exclusively uses `sidecar.norm_id` for candidate-vs-current finding matching in def 7 (both new-blocking and new-advisory detection), but `norm_id` bakes the sorted `criteria` list into its hash (`sidecar.py:268`). For R1, which extends the G6/E4 rubric by adding or restructuring criteria, a finding with identical text but altered criteria assignment will mint a different `norm_id`, making it appear as a NEW blocking or advisory finding even though the underlying defect is the same one the current gate already surfaces. This directly inflates `new_blocking_median` and `new_advisory_median_delta`, risking a false R1 FAIL on a sound rubric change. The codebase already provides `sidecar.fix_unit_key` (criteria-free, location+finding-text fingerprint, `sidecar.py:279-290`) precisely for cases where criterion identity must not affect deduplication.
findings.location: def 7 — LLM nondeterminism control (precise tier); also def 6 criterion_deltas schema
findings.suggested_fix: In `e3_metrics.fp_control_eval`, replace `sidecar.norm_id(f)` with `sidecar.fix_unit_key(f)` in the set-difference computations for `new_adv_i(t)` and `blocked_i(t)` (the candidate-vs-current finding comparisons). Keep `norm_id` for `criterion_deltas` (def 6) where per-criterion granularity is the point. Update the golden tests in `test_e3_metrics.py` to exercise a scenario where a finding keeps identical text but changes criteria, confirming it is NOT counted as new under fix_unit_key.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
